nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef crests exposed to moderate surge, typically among branching corals such as Pocillopora and Acropora species; depth range 3–15 m

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and other frozen carnivore preparations in captivity, though target feeding is generally advised in reef systems. Benefits from multiple small feedings per day when kept in bare-bottom tanks.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by jeyre; 2048x1535. Sharp, well-lit in-situ shot of a coral croucher wedged among branching Pocillopora, clearly showing the light gray/purplish body with numerous small reddish-brown and dark spots. Best diagnostic and technical quality.
Photo by Debra Baker; 2048x1536. Sharp full-body lateral view showing the spotted pattern and fuzzy skin texture; strong yellow lighting cast. Different individual for variety.
Photo by Debra Baker; 2048x1959. Whole fish wedged in coral with fins spread, spotting clearly visible; green-yellow color cast. Alternate angle.

summary: The Spotted Coral Croucher Goby is a small, sedentary member of the scorpionfish family from Indo-Pacific reef crests, where it wedges itself among branching Pocillopora and Acropora corals. Adults reach about 5 cm. Generally considered peaceful toward other species, though its dorsal spines are venomous and require careful handling.
careNotes:
The Spotted Coral Croucher Goby (Caracanthus maculatus) belongs to the family Scorpaenidae despite its goby-like appearance — it is not a true goby. The species tends to wedge itself among branching corals such as Pocillopora and Acropora and rarely swims in open water, making it best housed in a well-established reef with plenty of branching coral structure.

Handle with extreme caution and avoid touching the fish.

Reef safety: Generally considered reef-safe, but spawning individuals have been observed laying eggs on SPS coral tissue, which can cause localized tissue recession around the clutch site. Monitor closely in SPS-dominant systems.

Conspecific aggression: Individuals have been known to fight with their own kind in smaller tanks; the species is best kept singly or as a mated pair in larger reef setups.

Feeding: Target feeding is generally advised in reef systems, as the species tends not to compete well with faster tankmates. Once acclimated, most specimens have been reported to accept frozen mysis shrimp and similar small carnivore foods.

The body coloration is tan to light gray with numerous small reddish-brown spots, and the skin has small hair-like appendages that give the fish a fuzzy appearance. The species also exhibits a facultative air-breathing capability.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity, with eggs deposited on SPS coral tissue (notably Pocillopora). Captive breeding is uncommon and not well-documented in the hobby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.0 cm TL confirmed), native range (broader Indo-Pacific range vs LiveAquaria's Indonesia/GBR), and wild temperature habitat (25.4–29.3°C vs LiveAquaria's 72–78°F / 22–26°C). Temperature averaged to 24–27°C. FishBase range (India to Pacific Islands, north to Japan) used as more authoritative.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn on SPS coral tissue in captivity; full captive breeding documentation is limited.
